questions.question: Which of the following characteristics best indicates a workflow that is a good candidate for automation with a Custom Agent?

options.text: A task that requires creative brainstorming and subjective judgment
options.explanation: Creative brainstorming is not rule‑based and is hard to automate.

options.text: A repetitive, rule‑based task that takes the same steps each time
options.explanation: Custom Agents excel at repetitive, rule‑based work that can be described in clear instructions.

options.text: A one‑off project that will never be repeated
options.explanation: One‑off tasks don’t benefit from automation because there’s no recurring need.

options.text: A task that involves confidential information that only a single person should see
options.explanation: While possible, sharing confidential data widely can create security risks; it’s not the primary criteria for automation suitability.

questions.question: When writing instructions for a Custom Agent, why is it recommended to use @mentions instead of vague references like “my knowledge base”?

options.text: Because @mentions automatically grant edit permissions to the agent
options.explanation: @mentions only point to a resource; they don’t change permissions by themselves.

options.text: Because @mentions give the agent a precise location to pull information from, reducing ambiguity
options.explanation: Using @mentions tells the agent exactly which page, database, or person to reference, making the instruction clearer.

options.text: Because @mentions are required for the agent to run on a schedule
options.explanation: Scheduling is independent of @mentions; they are about referencing resources.

options.text: Because @mentions make the agent run faster
options.explanation: Speed isn’t directly affected; clarity is the main benefit.

questions.question: Which trigger type would you choose for a Reporting Agent that needs to deliver a weekly summary every Monday morning?

options.text: An @mention trigger in Slack
options.explanation: @mention triggers fire when someone mentions the agent, not on a time schedule.

options.text: A new page added to a database trigger
options.explanation: That trigger reacts to content changes, not to a calendar event.

options.text: A scheduled trigger set for every Monday morning
options.explanation: Scheduled triggers run at defined times, perfect for weekly reports.

options.text: A message posted in a Slack channel trigger
options.explanation: Message triggers depend on user activity, not on a regular schedule.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of reviewing the Custom Agent’s instructions after the initial build?

options.text: To add as many features as possible before sharing
options.explanation: Adding unnecessary features can make the agent harder to maintain.

options.text: To refine edge cases, escalation paths, and ensure the agent behaves predictably
options.explanation: Reviewing lets you handle nuances and verify the agent follows the intended logic.

options.text: To change the agent’s name and icon for branding
options.explanation: Naming is useful but not the main reason for instruction review.

options.text: To automatically grant the agent admin rights across the workspace
options.explanation: Permissions are managed separately; instruction review focuses on behavior.

questions.question: Why should you grant edit access to a Custom Agent only for the tools it truly needs to modify?

options.text: Because edit access slows down the agent’s performance
options.explanation: Performance isn’t directly affected by permission level.

options.text: Because giving broader edit rights can create security and data integrity risks
options.explanation: Limiting edit permissions reduces the chance of unintended changes or data leaks.

options.text: Because Notion will block the agent from running if it has edit access
options.explanation: The agent can run with edit access; the issue is about security, not functionality.

options.text: Because edit access prevents the agent from reading other pages
options.explanation: Edit access includes read capability; the concern is over‑privileging, not loss of access.

questions.question: During testing, which practice helps ensure that a new Custom Agent does not disrupt existing workflows?

options.text: Activate all triggers immediately after creating the agent
options.explanation: Activating immediately can cause unintended actions before you’ve verified behavior.

options.text: Run the agent manually in a dedicated test channel or environment before enabling triggers
options.explanation: Manual runs in a safe space let you see output without affecting production.

options.text: Share the agent with the entire team right after the first run
options.explanation: Broad sharing before confidence can spread errors.

options.text: Skip the Activity log review to save time
options.explanation: The Activity log is essential for diagnosing issues during testing.

questions.question: What does the Activity tab show that is especially useful when troubleshooting a Custom Agent?

options.text: A list of all team members who have viewed the agent
options.explanation: Viewership isn’t tracked there; the tab logs runs and actions.

options.text: Each run’s trigger source, actions taken, and any errors encountered
options.explanation: The Activity tab records the full execution trail, helping pinpoint where something went wrong.

options.text: The total amount of time the agent has been active in the workspace
options.explanation: It doesn’t provide aggregate uptime; it focuses on individual runs.

options.text: A preview of future scheduled runs
options.explanation: Future schedules are configured elsewhere; the Activity tab is about past runs.

questions.question: When sharing a Custom Agent with teammates, which of the following statements is true?

options.text: Edits made by any teammate will only affect their personal view of the agent
options.explanation: Edits apply to the shared agent and affect all future runs for everyone.

options.text: Permissions for the agent’s own access to tools are independent of the sharing permissions you grant
options.explanation: The agent runs with its own access rights, so you must review both the agent’s permissions and the sharing settings.

options.text: You must give every teammate admin rights to use the agent
options.explanation: Admin rights are unnecessary; appropriate view or edit permissions suffice.

options.text: The agent’s name automatically updates to include every teammate’s name
options.explanation: Naming is manual; it doesn’t change based on sharing.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a well‑structured “if‑then” instruction for a Custom Agent?

options.text: If a question mentions billing, respond with a short summary and a link to the pricing page.
options.explanation: It clearly states a condition and the exact action, aligning with the recommended instruction style.

options.text: When someone asks about anything, just give a random answer.
options.explanation: The instruction is vague and doesn’t define a concrete action.

options.text: Whenever I feel like it, I want the agent to do something.
options.explanation: Subjective language and lack of a trigger make it unusable.

options.text: If the agent is bored, it should post a meme in #general.
options.explanation: The condition isn’t observable by the system; it’s not a valid trigger.

questions.question: Why might you start with a pre‑built template when creating a new Custom Agent?

options.text: Templates automatically give the agent unlimited access to all workspace content
options.explanation: Templates don’t affect permissions; you still need to configure access.

options.text: Templates provide a structured starting point that can be customized, saving time on initial setup
options.explanation: Using a template gives you a baseline of instructions and triggers that you can adapt to your specific workflow.

options.text: Templates prevent you from adding any custom triggers later
options.explanation: You can always modify or add triggers after using a template.

options.text: Templates lock the agent’s name so it can’t be changed
options.explanation: Agent naming remains editable regardless of template use.
